cases.primaryGoal: Clarify a panic-spectrum presentation without overpathologizing stress.
cases.synopsis: Avery arrived from the emergency department after a second episode of chest tightness, dizziness, and fear of dying during finals week. Medical workup was unremarkable, but Avery now avoids driving alone and scans constantly for the next attack.

roomVariant.environmentalCue: A half-zipped backpack sits beside the visitor chair with a water bottle and exam flashcards.

hotspots.label: Patient Interview
hotspots.title: Patient Interview
hotspots.summary: Direct report from Avery at bedside.
hotspots.detail: Avery describes abrupt surges of fear that peak within minutes, followed by chest tightness, trembling, and fear of losing control. Avery now worries daily about having another episode in class or on the highway.

hotspots.label: Intake Chart
hotspots.title: Intake Chart
hotspots.detail: Triage notes document recurrent episodes of shortness of breath, dizziness, and fear of dying. Vitals normalize after each episode, and no cardiac findings were identified.

hotspots.label: Observation Notes
hotspots.title: Observation Notes
hotspots.detail: Avery scans the doorway repeatedly, keeps one hand on a water bottle, and asks whether staff can stay nearby in case symptoms return.

hotspots.label: Medication / History Record
hotspots.title: Medication / History Record
hotspots.summary: Medication list and relevant medical history.
hotspots.detail: No current psychiatric medication. Avery reports sleep dropping to 4-5 hours nightly during exams and denies substance use other than occasional energy drinks.

hotspots.label: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.title: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.detail: A roommate reports Avery has started texting before every class, asking friends to confirm exit routes and stay available in case an attack starts.

hotspots.label: Environmental Observation
hotspots.title: Environmental Observation
hotspots.detail: A backpack checklist includes water bottle, mints, headphones, and a note reading 'sit by the exit.' The pattern suggests anticipatory anxiety and safety behaviors.

cases.presentingConcern: Persistent worry, fatigue, and muscle tension
cases.primaryGoal: Differentiate chronic generalized anxiety from overlapping stress reactions.
cases.synopsis: Marisol was referred after repeated PCP visits for headaches, insomnia, and muscle tension. She reports relentless worry across parenting, finances, health, and work, and says she rarely feels mentally off duty.

roomVariant.environmentalCue: A planner on the bedside table is filled with backup plans and highlighted reminders.

hotspots.label: Patient Interview
hotspots.title: Patient Interview
hotspots.detail: Marisol says there is always another thing to worry about and that relaxing feels irresponsible. She reports feeling keyed up most days and having difficulty shutting off her thoughts at bedtime.

hotspots.label: Intake Chart
hotspots.title: Intake Chart
hotspots.detail: Symptoms have been present for more than a year, with excessive worry across multiple domains, restlessness, irritability, muscle tension, and fatigue.

hotspots.label: Observation Notes
hotspots.title: Observation Notes
hotspots.detail: Marisol frequently apologizes for 'forgetting something important,' rubs her shoulders, and asks whether the care team has considered all possible follow-up scenarios.

hotspots.label: Medication / History Record
hotspots.title: Medication / History Record
hotspots.detail: PCP notes ruled out thyroid concerns and prescribed a short-term sleep aid after persistent insomnia. No history of panic episodes or compulsive rituals is documented.

hotspots.label: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.title: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.detail: Her spouse reports Marisol spends evenings cycling through worst-case possibilities for school, bills, and health appointments, even on calm days.

hotspots.label: Environmental Observation
hotspots.title: Environmental Observation
hotspots.summary: Clues from nearby belongings and setup.
hotspots.detail: The bedside planner includes contingency plans for routine errands, childcare pickups, and weather changes. The pattern reflects pervasive worry rather than a single fear trigger.

cases.presentingConcern: Social avoidance and fear of embarrassment
cases.primaryGoal: Assess whether the social fear pattern fits an anxiety disorder presentation.
cases.synopsis: Jordan was encouraged to seek evaluation after avoiding teaching demonstrations, staff meetings, and shared dining areas on campus. Jordan reports dread before presentations and intense fear of negative evaluation in routine social settings.

roomVariant.environmentalCue: A folded campus map marks alternate routes between classrooms and quiet stairwells.

hotspots.label: Patient Interview
hotspots.title: Patient Interview
hotspots.detail: Jordan says it feels safer to disappear than risk people noticing anxiety. Anticipatory dread builds for days before presentations, and Jordan avoids eating where others might watch.

hotspots.label: Intake Chart
hotspots.title: Intake Chart
hotspots.detail: Jordan has repeatedly asked colleagues to cover teaching demonstrations and office hours because of overwhelming fear of embarrassment and negative judgment.

hotspots.label: Observation Notes
hotspots.title: Observation Notes
hotspots.detail: Jordan speaks softly, avoids sustained eye contact, and flushes visibly when discussing classroom performance or being watched while eating.

hotspots.label: Medication / History Record
hotspots.title: Medication / History Record
hotspots.detail: There is no history of discrete unexpected panic attacks outside social exposure situations. Jordan denies developmental communication concerns and describes the problem primarily as fear of humiliation.

hotspots.label: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.title: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.detail: Jordan's supervisor reports strong written work but marked avoidance of meetings, demos, and informal networking events needed for the graduate assistant role.

hotspots.label: Environmental Observation
hotspots.title: Environmental Observation
hotspots.summary: Clues from nearby belongings and setup.
hotspots.detail: The campus map and a stack of presentation note cards emphasize escape planning and overpreparation for evaluation situations.

cases.primaryGoal: Examine a trauma-related symptom cluster in context.
cases.synopsis: Noah presented after repeated sleep disruption and strong physiologic reactions to smoke alarms following a fatal apartment fire response six months ago. Coworkers have noticed withdrawal, irritability, and increased call-outs since the incident.

roomVariant.environmentalCue: A sealed station patch envelope rests near the window, unopened.

hotspots.label: Patient Interview
hotspots.title: Patient Interview
hotspots.detail: Noah reports nightmares, irritability, and immediate sensory flashbacks when alarms sound. He says he feels emotionally distant from coworkers and avoids the station kitchen after difficult calls.

hotspots.label: Intake Chart
hotspots.title: Intake Chart
hotspots.detail: The chart documents direct trauma exposure during a fatal apartment fire response, followed by re-experiencing symptoms, hyperarousal, sleep disruption, and avoidance over a six-month span.

hotspots.label: Observation Notes
hotspots.title: Observation Notes
hotspots.detail: Noah startles at hallway noise, sits with his back to the wall, and becomes visibly tense when discussing smoke alarms and the incident scene.

hotspots.label: Medication / History Record
hotspots.title: Medication / History Record
hotspots.detail: Noah has no prior panic diagnosis. A recent sleep aid improved duration only slightly, and nightmares continue several nights per week.

hotspots.label: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.title: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.detail: A coworker reports Noah removed team photos from his locker, avoids communal meals, and has become emotionally blunted since the fire response.

hotspots.label: Environmental Observation
hotspots.title: Environmental Observation
hotspots.summary: Clues from nearby belongings and setup.
hotspots.detail: The unopened station patch envelope and turned-over photo frame suggest avoidance of reminders tied to the traumatic event.

cases.primaryGoal: Evaluate obsessive-compulsive features without exposing the answer key.
cases.synopsis: Tessa came in after urgent care treated skin irritation from frequent hand-washing. Tessa reports contamination fears, repeated checking, and rigid rituals that now delay work arrival and make shared spaces feel unsafe.

roomVariant.environmentalCue: Travel soap bottles and sealed wipes are lined up with labels facing outward.

hotspots.label: Patient Interview
hotspots.title: Patient Interview
hotspots.detail: Tessa says contamination thoughts feel irrational but overwhelming. Repeating hand-washing or checking brings short relief, then the fear returns.

hotspots.label: Intake Chart
hotspots.title: Intake Chart
hotspots.detail: Tessa is frequently late to work because leaving home requires repeated lock, stove, and contamination checks. The rituals consume significant time each day.

hotspots.label: Observation Notes
hotspots.title: Observation Notes
hotspots.detail: Tessa asks whether the chair was sanitized, uses hand gel multiple times during the visit, and becomes briefly calmer after each ritual.

hotspots.label: Medication / History Record
hotspots.title: Medication / History Record
hotspots.detail: There is no primary medical explanation for the distress. Tessa denies panic episodes and instead describes intrusive contamination thoughts followed by compulsive behavior.

hotspots.label: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.title: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.detail: A sibling reports soap use has accelerated, extra bottles are stored in every room, and family outings are delayed while Tessa repeats washing routines.

hotspots.label: Environmental Observation
hotspots.title: Environmental Observation
hotspots.summary: Clues from nearby belongings and setup.
hotspots.detail: The arrangement of wipes, extra soap, and a written counting sequence points to ritualized behavior intended to neutralize intrusive contamination fears.

cases.presentingConcern: Depressed mood and loss of interest
cases.primaryGoal: Assess mood symptoms, impairment, and rule-outs.
cases.synopsis: Elliot presented after two months of low mood, early morning waking, guilt, low energy, and withdrawal from family and church activities following several workplace stressors. Elliot says daily functioning now feels effortful and flat.

roomVariant.environmentalCue: A lesson planner with incomplete entries sits beside an untouched snack.

hotspots.label: Patient Interview
hotspots.title: Patient Interview
hotspots.detail: Elliot describes feeling slowed down, guilty, and disconnected from activities that usually matter. There is no report of recent elevated mood, reduced need for sleep, or impulsive episodes.

hotspots.label: Intake Chart
hotspots.title: Intake Chart
hotspots.detail: The chart notes a two-month period of low mood, reduced appetite, low energy, impaired concentration, and diminished interest across work, home, and faith-community roles.

hotspots.label: Observation Notes
hotspots.title: Observation Notes
hotspots.detail: Elliot speaks softly, pauses before answering, and shows limited facial reactivity. Psychomotor slowing is noticeable during the interaction.

hotspots.label: Medication / History Record
hotspots.title: Medication / History Record
hotspots.detail: No prior manic episodes are documented. Medical screening is unremarkable, and the current episode extends beyond a brief situational sadness response.

hotspots.label: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.title: Family or Collateral Note
hotspots.detail: A spouse reports Elliot has withdrawn from hobbies, limits eye contact, wakes early, and has eaten very little over the last eight weeks.

hotspots.label: Environmental Observation
hotspots.title: Environmental Observation
hotspots.summary: Clues from nearby belongings and setup.
hotspots.detail: The unfinished lesson planner and untouched snack suggest diminished energy, concentration, and appetite rather than anxious overpreparation.
